question.text: An Indian exporter expects to receive Kuwaiti Dinars in 3 months and anticipates that the Indian Rupee will appreciate. Which currency derivative strategy would best protect the exporter's position?

answer.text: Buy a put option to sell Kuwaiti Dinars
Buy a call option to buy Kuwaiti Dinars
Buy a call option to buy Indian Rupees
Sell a put option on Indian Rupees

explanation.text: **The Logic:** If the Indian Rupee appreciates, the Kuwaiti Dinar will be worth fewer Rupees when converted. The exporter needs to sell Dinars and buy Rupees. A put option on Dinars gives the right to sell Dinars at a predetermined rate, protecting against Rupee appreciation (Dinar depreciation). **The Trap:** Buying a call on Dinars would be appropriate if the exporter expected Dinars to appreciate, which is opposite to the scenario. The exporter needs protection against receiving fewer Rupees per Dinar. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Exporters with foreign currency receivables hedge against home currency appreciation. Importers with foreign currency payables hedge against home currency depreciation. Match the derivative to the risk.
